# The Extended Abstract Model with Risky Choices {#sec-m1-identifiability .unnumbered}

## 5.1 Enriching the choice domain --- a two-step structural argument {#sec-m1-twostep .unnumbered}

The extended model ("`m_1`") augments the uncertain-choice setup with risky
alternatives whose probabilities are objectively given. The justification has two
steps; Step 1 alone does *not* identify $\delta$ --- Step 2 closes the argument.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Step 1 ($\beta$-free risky expected utility).** A risky alternative $s$ carries
an objective lottery $\pi_s \in \Delta^{K-1}$ over the $K$ consequences, and its
expected utility is $\eta^{(r)}_s = \pi_s^\top \bm{\upsilon}$, which depends only
on the utilities $\bm{\upsilon}$ (hence $\delta$) and not on the beliefs $\beta$
--- it does not pass through the belief map
$\bm{\psi}_r = \mathrm{softmax}(\beta w_r)$. The risky block therefore supplies
information about $\bm{\upsilon}$ (hence $\delta$) that is *free of* $\beta$: the
gradient of the risky log-likelihood with respect to $\delta$ does not also
depend on $\beta$. By itself this supplies unconfounded gradients but not
invertibility.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Step 2 (lottery diversity $\Rightarrow$ identification).** Recovering the $K$
utilities from risky choices requires the lotteries to point in enough different
directions across the consequence space --- intuitively, at least $K$ lotteries
whose payoff profiles differ enough to solve for each utility. *Given sufficient
lottery diversity* --- the presented lotteries' differences span the simplex
tangent space, equivalently a spanning affinely independent subset of $K$
lotteries with the menus linked into a connected contrast graph
(§[5.5](#sec-delta-id)), which requires $S \geq K$ --- the $\beta$-free risky
information *identifies* $\delta$.
:::

*History (brief).* Knight [-@knight1921risk] distinguished risk from uncertainty;
von Neumann and Morgenstern [-@vonneumann1947] axiomatized expected utility
under risk; Savage [-@savage1954] gave SEU under uncertainty.
Anscombe and Aumann [-@anscombe1963definition] combined the two by considering
acts mapping states to lotteries, which lets one identify utilities from the
risky margin and beliefs from the state margin. Our `m_1` uses a *related
empirical strategy*: the connection is conceptual, not a direct application of
the Anscombe–Aumann representation theorem. The important part is Steps 1+2,
not the representation theorem.

## 5.2 The extended model {#sec-m1-model .unnumbered}

Augment the $M$ uncertain problems with $N$ risky problems built from $S$
distinct lotteries $\pi_s$ (objective simplexes over the $K$ consequences). The
model shares the same $\alpha$ and the same $\delta \to \bm{\upsilon}$ map across
both blocks; risky expected utilities $\eta^{(r)}_s = \pi_s^\top \bm{\upsilon}$
depend only on $\delta$, not on $\beta$. (In the Stan code the lottery simplices
enter the `data` block as `x` and are compacted per problem into `x_risky`; we
use $\pi$ in the body.)

## 5.3 Preservation of the three sensitivity properties {#sec-m1-properties .unnumbered}

The softmax properties of §[2.3](#sec-three-properties) are properties of the
choice rule given *any* value function, so they apply unchanged to both uncertain
and risky sub-problems. Therefore the interpretation of $\alpha$ as
sensitivity-to-SEU-maximization is preserved in the extended model.

::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"}
**Single-$\alpha$ assumption.** `m_1`
asserts a *single* $\alpha$ governing both sub-problems --- i.e., the agent's
responsiveness to expected-utility differences does not depend on whether
probabilities are objective or subjective. The motivation is that $\alpha$ is
meant to capture one disposition --- the care an agent brings to acting on
expected-utility differences --- which one would expect to be a property of the
agent rather than of whether the probabilities it faces happen to be objective or
subjective. This is a substantive, testable
assumption, and it is essential to the §[6.4](#sec-m1-recovery)
interpretation of the matched recovery contrasts: the
quantity-versus-type reading of those contrasts requires a *common* $\alpha$
scale across blocks. If $\alpha$ differed across blocks, the matched comparison
would conflate a choice-*type* effect with a *block-mix* effect on the effective
$\alpha$-precision. The generalization with separate
$\alpha_{\text{unc}}, \alpha_{\text{risky}}$ (the companion model `m_2`) is named
but not pursued here. **Consequently, every conclusion that compares $\alpha$
across the uncertain and risky blocks is reported as explicitly conditional on
this single-$\alpha$ assumption**; the assumption is not verified at the prior
level (a genuine test would be posterior-predictive, §[6.3](#sec-m1-prior)), so
the conditional phrasing is mandatory, not optional.
:::

## 5.4 Identifiability of $\alpha$ in the extended model {#sec-m1-alpha .unnumbered}

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Proposition 5.1 (Identifiability of $\alpha$).** Proposition 3.1 holds
unchanged when $\eta$ is composed of both uncertain and risky alternatives: if
some menu of positive design probability has non-constant $\eta$, then
$\alpha > 0$ is identifiable from the choice-probability function.
:::

## 5.5 Identifiability of $\delta$ in the extended model {#sec-delta-id .unnumbered}

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Proposition 5.2 (Identifiability of $\delta$).** If the presented lotteries'
differences $\{\pi_s - \pi_1\}$ span the $(K-1)$-dimensional simplex tangent
space --- equivalently, the design contains an affinely independent subset of
$K$ lotteries (requiring $S \geq K$ and a generic configuration) linked by a
connected menu-contrast graph --- then $\alpha > 0$ and $\delta$ are jointly and
globally identifiable from risky-choice probabilities alone.
:::

*Intuition (full proof in Appendix B.3).* A risky menu's softmax depends on the
linear functional $\pi_s^\top \bm{\upsilon}$. Pinning down $K$ utilities requires
$K-1$ independent lottery directions (one degree of freedom is fixed by the
endpoint convention); with enough diverse lotteries those directions are present.
Formally, when the lottery differences span
the simplex tangent space, the map $\bm{\upsilon} \mapsto ((\pi_s - \pi_1)^\top
\bm{\upsilon})_s$ has rank $K-1$, so the risky log-odds recover $\bm{\upsilon}$
--- and jointly $\alpha$ --- once the endpoint convention fixes the scale, hence
$\delta$. With $K = 3$ and $S = 15$ (Appendix D.0) the spanning condition is
amply satisfied: 15 lotteries far exceed the $K = 3$ needed for a spanning
affinely independent subset. The proof is a linear-algebra inversion.

## 5.6 $\beta$ remains an irreducible nuisance {#sec-m1-beta .unnumbered}

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Proposition 5.3 ($\beta$ as an irreducible nuisance).** With $\delta$ pinned by
risky choices and $\alpha$ pinned by either block, the uncertain-choice
probabilities identify only the expected utilities $\eta_r = \bm{\psi}_r^\top
\bm{\upsilon}$ --- and these only up to a per-menu additive constant (Appendix
B.4). For $K \geq 3$ these scalars do not determine the belief
simplices $\bm{\psi}_r$, so $\beta$ is not point-identified beyond the level sets
of $\beta \mapsto (\eta_r)_r$ together with the row-shift gauge of
§[2.6](#sec-notation); it remains a nuisance even in the extended model.
:::

Once $\delta$ (hence $\bm{\upsilon}$) is fixed, the uncertain-choice likelihood
constrains $\beta$ only through the scalar expected utilities $\eta_r =
\bm{\psi}_r^\top \bm{\upsilon}$. Because a scalar does not recover the
$(K-1)$-dimensional simplex $\bm{\psi}_r$ when $K \geq 3$, the contribution of
the risky block is to sharpen $\bm{\upsilon}$, not to recover $\beta$ (Appendix
B.4).

## 5.7 The conceptual upshot --- and its finite-sample caveat {#sec-m1-upshot .unnumbered}

Enriching the choice domain with risky alternatives yields, *in principle*,
identification of $\alpha$ (from either block) and of the utilities $\delta$
(from the $\beta$-free risky block, Step 1, plus lottery diversity, Step 2). It
does *not* deliver the beliefs: $\beta$ enters uncertain choices only through the
scalar expected utilities and remains an irreducible nuisance for $K \geq 3$
(Proposition 5.3). And even the utility gain is an in-principle one --- it
implies estimability only *in the limit*.
§[6](#sec-m1-implementation) asks whether the gain is realized at
realistic finite sample sizes, and finds that for $\delta$ it largely is not ---
the cleanest illustration in the paper of the gap between identifiability and
precise estimability.
